reasoning: Bussen = the bus; stanser her = stops here. So (C) The bus stops here.
Jeg har = I have; en bil = a car. So (J) I have a car.
Bilen = the car; stanser her = stops here. So (E) The car stops here.
Jeg har = I have; eplet = the apple (neuter -et). So (H) I have the apple.
Jeg har = I have; et eple = an apple (neuter -et). So (B) I have an apple.
En kvinne = a woman (fem.); eplet = the apple. So (A) A woman has the apple.
Kvinna = the woman (fem. def. -a); biler = cars (indef. pl. -er). So (D) The woman has cars.
Kvinna = the woman; bilene = the cars (def. pl. -ene). So (G) The woman has the cars.
Jeg har = I have; busser = buses (indef. pl. -er). So (F) I have buses.
Kvinnene = the women (def. pl. -ene); stanser her = stop here. So (I) The women stop here.
Feminine definite uses -a: jenta; verb ‘stanser’ unchanged; ‘her’ = here.
Indef. fem. takes en: en jente; neuter indef. takes et: et hotell.
Definite plural takes -ene: hundene = the dogs.
Indefinite plural commonly takes -er: hunder; subject in definite fem.: jenta.
Mennene = the men (mann → menn → mennene); epler = apples (neuter indef. pl.).

answer: The woman does not have the shoes
reasoning: Kvinna = the woman; ikke = not; skoene = the shoes (def. pl.).

answer: I do not have the apples
reasoning: Jeg har ikke = I do not have; eplene = the apples (def. pl. -ene).

answer: Norwegian marks definiteness with suffixes: common gender -en (bilen), neuter -et (eplet), and feminine often -a (kvinna). Indefinite articles are en for common/feminine and et for neuter. Indefinite plural typically adds -er (biler, hunder; neuter eple → epler), and the definite plural adds -ene (bilene, eplene, kvinnene, skoene). Verbs like stanser do not change with person/number. Negation is ikke placed after the verb har.
reasoning: Summarizes the morphological rules used to derive all matches and translations without step-by-step internal deliberation.
